title: Mutable Default Argument
description: Using a mutable object (list, dict, set) as a default argument value. The same object is shared across all calls — mutations persist between calls.
symptoms: A list or dict default parameter accumulates values across separate function calls. Results differ on the second call vs the first even with the same inputs.
code_smell: def f(x, items=[]) or def f(x, config={}) — mutable object in the signature default
fix: Use None as the default and create the mutable object inside the function body.

title: Late Binding in Loop Closures
description: Lambda or nested functions created inside a loop capture the loop variable by reference, not by value. All closures see the variable's final value after the loop ends.
symptoms: A list of lambdas or callbacks all produce the same output — the last value the loop variable held.
code_smell: funcs = [lambda: i for i in range(5)] — every lambda returns 4
fix: Capture the current value as a default argument: lambda i=i: i
example_fix:
funcs = [lambda i=i: i for i in range(5)]
# Now funcs[0]() == 0, funcs[4]() == 4

title: Bare Except Clause Swallows All Errors
description: Using 'except:' without specifying an exception type catches everything, including KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit, and GeneratorExit. Critical signals are silenced.
symptoms: Ctrl+C does not interrupt the program. Bugs are hidden because every exception is swallowed silently.
code_smell: try: ... except: pass or try: ... except: continue
fix: Always name the specific exception type(s) you intend to handle.

title: Identity Check Used for Value Comparison
description: Using 'is' or 'is not' to compare integers, strings, or booleans. 'is' tests object identity (same memory address), not value equality.
symptoms: Comparison returns False for values that look equal, or returns True inconsistently depending on Python's object interning.
code_smell: if x is 5: or if name is 'player1': — 'is' used with non-None values
fix: Use == for value comparison. Reserve 'is' exclusively for None checks.
example_fix:
if x == 5:          # value comparison — correct
if name == 'player': # value comparison — correct
if result is None:   # identity check — the only correct use of 'is'

title: Floor Division vs True Division Confusion
description: In Python 3, the / operator always returns a float even when both operands are integers. Code expecting integer results (scores, indices, counts) silently receives floats.
symptoms: A score or counter gets an unexpected .0 suffix. Comparisons against int values fail because 5.0 != 5 in strict type checking.
code_smell: score = total / rounds — returns float when int is expected
fix: Use // for integer (floor) division when a whole-number result is required.
example_fix:
score = total_points // num_rounds  # integer division
ratio = total_points / num_rounds   # float division — intentional

title: Shadowing a Python Built-in Name
description: Naming a variable the same as a Python built-in (list, dict, input, id, type, range, etc.) hides the built-in for the rest of the scope.
symptoms: TypeError: 'list' object is not callable, or unexpected behaviour when code that relied on the built-in runs later in the same scope.
code_smell: list = [1, 2, 3] or input = st.text_input(...) or id = record.id
fix: Rename the variable to avoid collision with the built-in: items, user_input, record_id.
example_fix:
# Problematic:
list = get_scores()
print(list([1, 2]))  # TypeError!

# Fixed:
scores = get_scores()
print(list([1, 2]))  # works fine

title: Mutating a List While Iterating Over It
description: Adding or removing items from a list inside a for loop that iterates over the same list causes elements to be skipped or visited twice.
symptoms: Some items are never processed, the loop terminates early, or it iterates more times than the list length.
code_smell: for item in my_list: my_list.remove(item) or my_list.append(new_item)
fix: Iterate over a copy of the list, or collect changes and apply them after the loop.

title: Unguarded Dictionary Key Access
description: Accessing a dictionary key directly with dict[key] raises KeyError if the key is absent. No default handling causes crashes on unexpected input.
symptoms: KeyError exception when a key doesn't exist. Program crashes on first missing or unexpected dictionary entry.
code_smell: config['difficulty'] or data['score'] without existence check
fix: Use dict.get(key, default) for optional keys, or check 'key in dict' before accessing.
